title: Why Schema-Guided Memory Makes Agents More Reliable
thesis: Defining a structured ontology before storing memories enables agents to build precise, queryable knowledge graphs that resist hallucination and outdated information.
audience: Developers and AI practitioners building agent memory systems

glossary.definition: A formal specification of entity types, attributes, and relationships allowed in the memory graph.
A Python library that defines data schemas with validation; used here to enforce memory structure.
The process of merging different mentions (e.g., 'OpenAI', 'Open AI') into a single canonical entity.
The property that facts are associated with time intervals, allowing outdated beliefs to be ignored rather than overwritten.
A graph whose nodes represent entities and edges represent relationships, often with typed attributes.
A predefined structure that dictates what types of information can be stored and how they relate.
A rule limiting which entity types can be connected by a specific relationship, e.g., only 'Person' can 'own' a 'Project'.

visual_style.palette: pastel pink, lavender, mint green, pale yellow, soft blue
visual_style.character: a small robot with a blueprint roll
visual_style.recurring_metaphor: architect's blueprint or scaffolding that imposes order on a chaotic pile of sticky notes

sections.heading: The Problem: Unstructured Memory Fails
sections.purpose: Explain why leaving memory structure open-ended leads to vague entities, high noise, and unreliable queries.
key_points: Raw text stored without schema lets the LLM invent vague entity types like 'Topic' and generic edges like 'RELATES_TO'.
Long context increases noise, duplicates facts, and lets outdated statements compete with current ones.
Without structure, precise queries cannot reliably retrieve the right information.

sections.heading: Define an Ontology First
sections.purpose: Show that defining entity types, attributes, and descriptions before information extraction ensures consistent, searchable memory.
key_points: Pydantic models pre-define allowed entity types and typed attributes.
The extraction model searches for domain-specific concepts instead of guessing a schema on the fly.
Edge definitions constrain which entity types may be connected, rejecting invalid relationships early.

sections.heading: Resolve Entities Over Time
sections.purpose: Explain how entity resolution and temporal fields keep the graph consistent and up-to-date.
key_points: Entity resolution merges different surface forms (e.g., 'OpenAI', 'Open AI') into a single node.
Temporal fields track when a fact is valid, distinguishing current from outdated truths.
New facts invalidate older ones within a validity window without silent overwriting.

sections.heading: The Result: A Queryable Model of the Domain
sections.purpose: Summarize the benefits: the schema acts as both a storage contract and a reasoning boundary.
key_points: A schema-guided temporal knowledge graph becomes a precise, queryable model of the domain.
The schema is both a storage contract and a reasoning boundary: relationships that cannot be represented cannot be casually hallucinated.
Memory remains reliable, up-to-date, and resistant to noise.
